topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed a monotonous tune that usually faded into the background noise of my workday. Today, however, every vibration seemed amplified, a low thrumming beneath my skin. I was reviewing quarterly reports, my fingers hovering over the spreadsheet, but my eyes kept drifting toward the corner of the screen where Sarah’s name appeared. A cold knot tightened in my stomach as I cross-referenced her salary data with mine. The numbers didn't lie. The disparity was stark, a chasm yawning between our paychecks. I consciously slowed my breathing, forcing my gaze back to the figures, trying to keep my expression neutral, professional. Every click of the mouse felt loud in the sudden quiet of my concentration.

[story 2]

I took a long sip of lukewarm coffee, the bitter taste doing little to settle the churning in my gut. My jaw felt tight, and I kept tapping my pen against the desk, a frantic, rhythmic sound against the silence of the open-plan workspace. It started innocently enough—a misplaced invoice, a casual conversation overheard in the breakroom—but the pieces clicked into place with alarming speed. I felt a sudden, sharp need to verify everything, to confirm the unsettling reality. My hands were clammy as I pulled up the internal compensation structure, the screen reflecting a sheen of sweat on my forehead. A slow, deliberate movement brought me to the conclusion, and the air seemed to thin around me.

[story 3]

The silence in my cubicle felt pressurized, heavy, like the air before a storm breaks. I stared at the framed motivational poster on the wall, the cheerful imagery mocking the sudden, unwelcome realization blooming in my chest. My pulse was quickening, a frantic drumbeat I could feel vibrating in my ears. I re-read the performance metrics, the achievements listed next to each employee. When I finally compared the figures, the discrepancy was undeniable, a glaring error in my understanding of the company's structure. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly across the linoleum floor. My voice, when I finally spoke to myself, was unnervingly steady, though my hands were clenched so tightly my knuckles were white.
